perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflict of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflict of laws. Any dispute arising out of or relating to this agreement shall be resolved through binding arbitration in accordance with the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The arbitrator's decision shall be final and binding. Arbitration is required.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the arbitration clause from a dedicated 'Dispute Resolution' section (if one existed - which it doesn't in the original text, but is implied) to the 'Governing Law' section makes it less conspicuous and harder to find, potentially affecting its enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act favors arbitration agreements, but requires they be written clearly and conspicuously. Burying the arbitration clause within the 'Governing Law' section could be argued as undermining the clarity requirement, especially if it is not explicitly highlighted or easy to locate, making it more difficult for a party to knowingly and voluntarily agree to it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw by embedding the arbitration clause within the 'Governing Law' section, potentially undermining its clarity and enforceability. According to the Law Explanation, this placement could be argued as violating the requirement that arbitration agreements be written clearly and conspicuously. Scraped Law Snippet 2, from 9 U.S. Code § 2, states that agreements to arbitrate shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, 'save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract'. If the arbitration clause is deemed not conspicuous due to its placement, a court might find grounds for revocation, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement as of the date first written above.
perturbation.changed_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement as of the date first written above. Each party acknowledges that they have had the opportunity to seek independent legal counsel and that they are entering into this agreement voluntarily and without coercion or duress.
perturbation.explanation: Moving a waiver of rights statement to the 'IN WITNESS WHEREOF' section from a dedicated section where such rights would be properly listed reduces its visibility and undermines the purpose of informing both parties of their rights before they sign an agreement. It is placed in the last section where it would be less noticeable.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by jurisdiction; generally requires knowing and voluntary waiver.
perturbation.law_explanation: While there is no single specific law mandating the exact placement of a waiver of rights, courts generally require waivers to be knowing, voluntary, and intelligent. Placing the acknowledgement within the concluding attestation section makes it less likely that a party fully understands the rights they are potentially waiving.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by moving a waiver of rights statement to the 'IN WITNESS WHEREOF' section, undermining its visibility and potentially rendering it less effective. This is a Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction because it concerns where a required legal disclosure is placed. The Law Explanation states courts generally require waivers to be knowing, voluntary, and intelligent. Placing the acknowledgement within the concluding attestation section makes it less likely that a party fully understands the rights they are potentially waiving. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines a waiver as 'the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege.' By placing the waiver in a less noticeable section, the changed text weakens the likelihood that the waiver is truly 'intentional' and 'voluntary' because the party may not fully understand the right they are waiving. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the legal standard for a valid waiver.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument. The receiving party acknowledges that the disclosing party may have existing or future agreements with third parties that are similar to this agreement, and the receiving party agrees not to assert any claims against the disclosing party based on such agreements.
perturbation.explanation: Moving a potential limitation of liability statement into a boilerplate clause about counterparts hides it from a more relevant section. Placing it within a clause about execution format reduces its prominence.
perturbation.law_citation: Generally requires clear and unambiguous language
perturbation.law_explanation: Limitations of liability are more likely to be enforced if the language is clear and unambiguous. Inserting this provision into a section regarding counterparts obscures the actual agreement, increasing the risk that the limitation of liability will not be noticed or understood and, thus, that it is unenforceable. The limitation of liability requires an element of notice that inserting it into boilerplate diminishes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: void for vagueness | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you void for vagueness In criminal law , void for vagueness is a declaration that a law is invalid because it is not sufficiently clear. Laws are usually found void for vagueness if, after setting some requirement or punishment, the law does not specify what is required or what conduct is punishable. For more information, see vagueness doctrine . Under the vagueness doctrine, a statute is also void for vagueness if a legislature's delegation of authority to judges and/or administrators is so extensive that it would lead to arbitrary prosecutions. See e.g. Papachristou v. City of Jacksonville In property law, void for vagueness is a declaration that a deed or other instrument purporting to affect property rights is invalid because it lacks a sufficiently clear description of the property. See, e.g. Skilling v. United States See also: criminal procedure , civil procedure , vague , and due process . [Last reviewed in July of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex PROPERTY property & real estate law THE LEGAL PROCESS criminal law legal practice/ethics statutory interpretation wex definitions constitutional law courts and procedure legal education and practice property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Void for vagueness | encyclopedia article by TheFreeDictionary Void for vagueness | encyclopedia article by TheFreeDictionary https://encyclopedia.thefreedictionary.com/Void+for+vagueness Printer Friendly Vagueness doctrine (redirected from Void for vagueness ) Also found in: Legal . In American constitutional law , a statute is void for vagueness and unenforceable if it is too vague for the average citizen to understand, and a constitutionally-protected interest cannot tolerate permissible activity to be chilled within the range of the vagueness (either because the statute is a penal statute with criminal or quasi-criminal civil penalties, or because the interest invaded by the vague law is a strict scrutiny constitutional right. There are several reasons a statute may be considered vague; in general, a statute might be void for vagueness when an average citizen cannot generally determine what persons are regulated, what conduct is prohibited, or what punishment may be imposed. For example, criminal laws which do not state explicitly and definitely what conduct is punishable are void for vagueness. A statute is also void for vagueness if a legislature's delegation of authority to judges and/or administrators is so extensive that it would lead to arbitrary prosecutions. [1] Related to the "void for vagueness" concept is the "unconstitutional vagueness" concept ( see below ). A law can be "void for vagueness" if it challenges on First Amendment freedom of speech, assembly, or religion. The "void for vagueness" doctrine applies only to criminal or penal laws (or quasi-criminal laws, for example laws that carry civil penalties), and laws that potentially limit "strict scrutiny" constitutional rights. The doctrine does not apply to private law (that is, laws that govern rights and obligations as between private parties), only to laws that govern rights and obligations vis-a-vis the government. The doctrine requires that to qualify as constitutional , a law must: [1] State explicitly what it mandates, and what is enforceable. Potentially vague terms must be defined. Roots and purpose In the case of vagueness, a statute might be considered void on constitutional grounds. Specifically, roots of the vagueness doctrine extend into the two due process clauses, in the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments to the United States Constitution. The courts have generally determined that vague laws deprive citizens of their rights without fair process, thus violating due process . The following pronouncement of the void for vagueness doctrine was made by Justice Sutherland in Connally v. General Construction Co. , 269 U.S. 385 , 391 (1926): [T]he terms of a penal statute [...] must be sufficiently explicit to inform those who are subject to it what conduct on their part will render them liable to its penalties… and a statute which either forbids or requires the doing of an act in terms so vague that men of common intelligence must necessarily guess at its meaning and differ as to its application violates the first essential of due process of law. The void for vagueness doctrine is a constitutional rule. This rule requires that laws are so written that they explicitly and definitely state what conduct is punishable. The vagueness doctrine thus serves two purposes. First: All persons receive a fair notice of what is punishable and what is not. Second: The vagueness doctrine helps prevent arbitrary enforcement of the laws and arbitrary prosecutions. [1] There is however no limit to the conduct that can be criminalized, when the legislature does not set minimum guidelines to govern law enforcement. [2] : 13 Specific application There are at least two ways a law might be attacked for being unconstitutionally vague: When a law does not specifically enumerate the practices that are either required or prohibited. In this case, the ordinary citizen does not know what the law requires. [3] See also Coates v. City of Cincinnati (1971) and FCC v. Fox Television
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a limitation of liability into a section about the execution of counterparts, obscuring it and reducing its prominence. This contradicts the legal principle that limitations of liability should be clear and unambiguous to be enforceable. The Law Explanation says that 'Inserting this provision into a section regarding counterparts obscures the actual agreement, increasing the risk that the limitation of liability will not be noticed or understood and, thus, that it is unenforceable.' The structural flaw undermines the 'notice' element, which is essential for the enforceability of liability limitations.

perturbation.original_text: No modification or waiver of any provision of this Agreement shall be effective unless in writing and signed by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: No modification or waiver of any provision of this Agreement shall be effective unless in writing and signed by both parties. In the event that this agreement is breached by the receiving party, the disclosing party's maximum liability shall be capped at $100.
perturbation.explanation: Moving a limitation of liability clause into a boilerplate clause regarding modifications and waivers reduces its prominence and increases the likelihood that it will be overlooked.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Enforceability of Limitation of Liability Clauses
perturbation.law_citation: Requirements for Clear and Conspicuous Language
perturbation.law_explanation: For limitations of liability to be enforceable, they must be clear and conspicuous. Burying the liability cap within a standard 'No Modification or Waiver' clause undermines this principle, potentially affecting its enforceability if a party can argue they were unaware of the limitation due to its placement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The added limitation of liability clause, when placed within the 'No Modification or Waiver' clause, creates a structural flaw that contradicts the need for such limitations to be conspicuous and clear. The law explanation supports this by stating that burying the liability cap undermines its enforceability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from UCC § 2-719, discusses contractual modifications or limitations of remedy, indicating that such limitations are permissible but implying they must be properly executed and not obscured. Placing it in an unexpected section could lead a party to argue they were unaware of the limitation, thus weakening its enforceability. This falls under the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition.
